1: Jehoash (Yəhôʾāš, YHWH-has-given) becomes king in Jehu's seventh year — the synchronism anchors him in the Jehu dynasty of Israel while his long forty-year reign in Jerusalem marks him as the most durable of the Athaliah-crisis survivors.
2: ʾăšer hôrāhû Yəhôyāḏāʿ hakkōhēn — 'because Jehoiada the priest instructed him.' The syntax makes Joash's righteousness contingent (kol-yəmê ʾăšer hôrāhû, 'all his days, to the extent that Jehoiada instructed him'); where the mentorship ends, the good ends with it (cf. vv20-21 — apostasy and assassination after Jehoiada's death in 2 Chronicles 24:17-22).
3: habbāmôt lōʾ-sārû — 'the high places were not removed.' The bāmôt qualification recurs in every otherwise-positive regnal evaluation in Kings; it marks the limit of the Josianic standard these pre-Josiah kings cannot reach and signals that the cult is still partially decentralized despite Jerusalem's central sanctuary.
4: Three categories of temple income: (1) kesef ʿōbēr — 'money from those who pass over,' the half-shekel census/poll tax of Exodus 30:12-16; (2) kesef nepeš ʿerěkô — 'money of the soul of one's valuation,' the Levitical redemption assessments of Lev 27; (3) kesef yāḇîʾ ʾîš ʿal libbô — 'money any man brings on his heart,' i.e., freewill offerings. The classification shows Joash understood the three legally distinct funding streams and intended a systematic collection.
5: yəḥazzəqû ʾeṯ-beḏeq habbayiṯ — 'let them repair the breaches of the house.' beḏeq (breach, crack) is the structural term for damage to load-bearing walls; the priests are to collect the money and disburse it to the craftsmen who repair the actual stone and timber.
6: By the twenty-third year of Joash the priests had still not repaired the temple breaches — the system of v5 failed, either from priestly diversion of funds or administrative neglect. The failure prompts Joash's reform of the collection system in vv9-16.
7: Joash summons Jehoiada and the priests and prohibits them from receiving money from their acquaintances (mimmayyuddaʿăkhem) and from the repair obligation. The king does not punish for past failure but restructures the system going forward — a pragmatic rather than punitive response.
8: The priests agree to neither receive money nor repair the temple — they yield the fiscal responsibility back to the crown. This transfer of temple finances from priestly to royal oversight is a significant administrative shift in the relationship between palace and sanctuary.
9: Jehoiada the priest bored a hole (wayyiqboʿ ḥōr) in the lid of the chest (ʾārôn) and set it beside the altar at the right of the entrance. The gatekeeping priests put all the money brought into YHWH's house into it — a transparent, auditable collection system replacing the priestly ad hoc arrangement.
10: When the chest was full, the king's secretary and the high priest would count the silver and wrap it in bags. The dual-oversight system (royal secretary + high priest) provides accountability — neither party handles money alone.
11: The weighed-out money was given directly to the overseers (pəqûḏîm, the appointed ones) of the work on YHWH's house, who disbursed to carpenters and builders, masons and stonecutters, timber-buyers and hewn-stone-buyers. The craftsmen received wages directly, bypassing priestly intermediaries.
12: All the skilled trades required for temple restoration are listed — carpenters, builders, masons, stonecutters — plus quarried stone (ʾaḇnê māḥṣāḇ) and timber for the structural repairs. The enumeration signals both the scope and the competence of the project.
13: A notable limitation: the silver was not used to make silver basins, snuffers, bowls, trumpets, or gold and silver vessels for YHWH's house. The repair project was strictly structural, not decorative — the priority was the building's fabric, not its furnishings.
14: The craftsmen used the money for repairs without any accounting to those who gave it, kî beʾĕmûnāh hêm ʿōśîm — 'because they worked in faithfulness/trustworthiness.' ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness) is the noun from ʾāman, which underlies the liturgical ʾāmēn; the craftsmen's integrity made detailed auditing unnecessary.
15: The same note as v14 — no accounting required because of the workers' ʾĕmûnāh. The double mention underscores that trustworthy stewardship makes bureaucratic oversight secondary; the integrity of the workers validates the entire project.
16: Guilt offering (ʾāšām) and sin offering (ḥaṭṭāʾṯ) money went to the priests separately — it was not deposited in the chest. The text thus distinguishes the two funding streams: building funds go into the chest; sacrificial-system proceeds remain in priestly hands. The two budgets do not mingle.
17: Hazael attacks Gath and turns to attack Jerusalem. Joash takes the qoḏāšîm (consecrated/sacred things) dedicated by his ancestors Jehoshaphat, Joram, Ahaziah, and himself, plus all the gold in the temple treasury, and sends them to Hazael. The bitter irony: the king who repaired the temple's structure strips its consecrated wealth to buy off a foreign threat — the temple budget built up over three reigns is dispatched in one payment.
18: wayyēleḵ mēʿal Yərûšālem — 'and he withdrew from Jerusalem.' The tribute payment works: Hazael turns away. But the cost was the sacred dedications, the gold reserves, and presumably the goodwill built by the temple repair project. The story implies that material security purchased at the cost of the sacred is not security at all.
19: The remaining acts of Joash are in the royal chronicle. His reign ends not with a full regnal account but with the assassination record that follows — the end is shaped by the murder, not by the building project.
20: Joash's servants conspire against him and strike him at bêṯ millôʾ hayyôrēḏ Sillāʾ — the house of Millo, which goes down to Silla. The location is unknown but it belongs to the Millo-complex, the fill-work of the older City of David. The assassination terminates a forty-year reign by a conspiracy among his own court.
21: The assassins are named: Jozachar son of Shimeath and Jehozabad son of Shomer. Both names and maternal pedigrees are given — the text does not conceal the perpetrators. Joash is buried with his fathers in the City of David; his son Amaziah succeeds him.
